title: Heartbeat Protection Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
plain_summary: This bill would make it a federal crime for doctors to perform abortions without first checking for a fetal heartbeat. If a heartbeat is found, doctors cannot perform the abortion except in special cases. The exceptions are when the mother's life is in danger from a physical health problem, or when the pregnancy happened because of rape or incest. Doctors who break this law could face up to five years in prison and fines. Women who get abortions cannot be charged with a crime under this law.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Heartbeat Protection Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Abortions Prohibited Without a Check for Fetal Heartbeat, or if a Fetal Heartbeat is Detectable
sections.text: This section changes federal criminal law by adding new rules about abortions. Any doctor who performs an abortion will commit a crime if they do any of these things. First, they perform an abortion without checking if the unborn child has a heartbeat using proper medical methods. Second, they do not tell the mother what they found when they checked for a heartbeat. Third, they perform an abortion after finding that the unborn child does have a heartbeat. If a doctor breaks these rules, they can be fined or go to prison for up to five years or both. These rules do not apply if the abortion is needed to save the mother's life when her life is in danger from a physical problem, physical sickness, or physical injury. This includes physical conditions caused by the pregnancy itself. However, mental or emotional problems do not count as reasons to allow the abortion. There are some exceptions to these rules. The law does not apply if a doctor decides the abortion is needed to save a pregnant woman's life when it is in danger from a physical problem, sickness, or injury, including conditions caused by the pregnancy. Mental or emotional conditions do not count. The law also does not apply if the pregnancy happened because an adult woman was raped, and at least forty eight hours before the abortion she got counseling for the rape or got medical treatment for the rape or injuries from the rape. Another exception is if the pregnancy happened because a minor was raped or a victim of incest, and the rape or incest was reported before the abortion to either a government agency that handles child abuse reports or a law enforcement agency. Doctors must keep certain paperwork to prove these exceptions apply. For adult women who were raped, the doctor must put documentation in the patient's medical file before doing the abortion. This documentation must come from a licensed hospital, licensed medical clinic, licensed personal doctor, licensed counselor, or a victim's rights advocate from law enforcement. The paperwork must show the woman got medical treatment or counseling for the rape or related injuries. For minors who were raped or victims of incest, the doctor must put documentation in the medical file before the abortion. This must come from a government agency that handles child abuse reports showing the rape or incest was reported before the abortion. As an alternative, it can come from law enforcement showing the rape or incest was reported. The law requires that certain medical records be kept according to existing federal rules about patient information. The counseling and medical treatment mentioned in the exceptions cannot be provided by a facility that performs abortions, unless that facility is a hospital. If the rape was already reported to law enforcement or Department of Defense victim assistance workers, then the forty eight hour counseling or treatment requirement does not apply. If a doctor is charged with breaking this law, they can ask for a hearing with the State Medical Board. The board will decide if the doctor's actions were necessary to save the mother's life from physical problems. The board's decision can be used as evidence in the criminal trial. The court must delay the trial for up to thirty days to allow this hearing if the doctor requests it. A mother who has an abortion cannot be prosecuted under this law. She cannot be charged with conspiracy or as an accessory either. The doctor must keep records showing whether they checked for a heartbeat, what they found, that they told the mother the results, and any information used in legal proceedings. These records must follow federal rules about keeping patient information. If any part of this law is found to be invalid by a court, the rest of the law will still be in effect. The law defines several important terms. Counseling means help provided by a state licensed counselor or a victim's rights advocate from law enforcement. Medical treatment means care given at a state licensed hospital, federal hospital, state licensed medical clinic, federal medical clinic, or by a state licensed personal doctor. Abortion means using any tool, medicine, drug, or device to intentionally kill an unborn child or end a pregnancy, except when trying to deliver a living baby after viability or remove a dead unborn child. An attempt at abortion means taking substantial steps toward performing an abortion. A facility includes any medical or counseling group, center, or clinic and the entire legal organization that controls it. To perform an abortion includes causing an abortion through medical or chemical means, including writing prescriptions for drugs or devices meant to cause abortion. A physician is someone licensed to practice medicine and surgery or legally authorized to perform abortions. Reasonable medical judgment means the medical decision a careful and knowledgeable doctor would make given the circumstances and treatment options. An unborn child means a human organism from fertilization until being born alive as defined in federal law. A woman means a female human being regardless of age. Nothing in this law prevents federal, state, or local laws that give greater protection to unborn children. Nothing in this law creates or recognizes a right to abortion or makes legal any abortion that was illegal when this law takes effect.
